one_line_takeaway: QIBP extends classical interval bound propagation to variational quantum classifiers via complex interval and affine arithmetic, achieving tight certified-vs-test-vs-PGD accuracy on small VQCs but showing bounds that loosen substantially as qubit and class counts grow.
key_finding: Under interval arithmetic (Table I, epsilon=0.001, kappa=0.5), small/shallow models achieve tight certified bounds -- e.g., MNIST, 4 qubits, 2 classes, 2 layers, margin loss: test accuracy 96.36% vs certified accuracy 95.37% (difference 0.99%), with PGD accuracy 96.12% closely matching. But certified accuracy degrades sharply as models scale up: MNIST, 10 qubits, 2 classes, 2 layers, margin loss shows a 39.71% gap between test (98.01%) and certified (58.30%) accuracy, even though PGD accuracy (97.87%) stays close to test accuracy -- indicating the interval bounds become loose (over-conservative), not that the model is actually non-robust. Under affine arithmetic (Table II, same epsilon/kappa), bounds are tighter: MNIST, 4 qubits, 2 classes, 8 layers, cross-entropy loss reaches 94.61% test / 94.42% certified accuracy; at 10 qubits, 2 classes, 8 layers it is 98.11% test / 92.34% certified. As classes increase to 10 (10 qubits, 8 layers), accuracies collapse to 20.82% test / 13.83% certified (cross-entropy) versus 10.84% test / 1.50% certified (margin loss) -- one of the cases where margin loss underperforms cross-entropy. Margin loss generally improves accuracy over cross-entropy loss (e.g., MNIST 6 qubits/6 classes/8 layers: cross-entropy 72.11% test/67.63% certified vs margin loss 82.47% test/77.76% certified) but is not universally better. Ablations on epsilon (Table III) show certified accuracy is far more sensitive to epsilon than test accuracy is (e.g., 8 qubits/2 classes/4 layers: certified accuracy falls from 99.15% at epsilon=0.001 to 70.64% at epsilon=0.010 while test accuracy stays near 98-99%), and more classes make this sensitivity worse (4 classes, 6 qubits/4 layers: certified accuracy falls from 85.21% to 4.62% as epsilon goes 0.001 to 0.010). Ablations on kappa (Table IV) show decreasing kappa from 0.9 toward 0.5/0.1 generally improves both test and certified accuracy, with most gains realized by kappa=0.5 and diminishing returns below that (e.g., 6-layer model: test accuracy 80.44%->89.01%->89.51% as kappa goes 0.9->0.5->0.1). Overall, certified and PGD accuracy remain tightly bounded for small/simple model configurations, but the certified-accuracy metric becomes loose relative to both test and empirical PGD accuracy as qubit count and class count grow, which the authors attribute to interval arithmetic's rectangular over-approximation (dependency problem) rather than to genuine loss of robustness.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Quantum Interval Bound Propagation (QIBP), a certified training method for variational quantum classifiers (VQCs) that adapts classical interval bound propagation (IBP) to the quantum domain. It propagates complex-valued interval bounds (real and imaginary parts) through the feature map, ansatz layers, and measurement stages of a VQC, in two variants: (1) interval arithmetic and (2) affine arithmetic (with Chebyshev-approximation handling of the nonlinear squaring step at measurement). During training, a two-term loss combines standard cross-entropy on clean logits with either a worst-case-logit cross-entropy term or a margin-based hinge loss computed from the propagated bounds, so the model is optimized to keep the lower bound of the correct class logit above the upper bounds of all other class logits under a given perturbation epsilon. The authors state this is, to their knowledge, the first certified-training (IBP-style) method for QML models.
threat_model: Adversarial perturbations of bounded magnitude epsilon (L-infinity-style interval perturbation x-epsilon to x+epsilon) applied to input images classified by a VQC, consistent with attacks such as FGSM and PGD referenced in the paper's background. The goal of QIBP is to certify -- via propagated interval/affine bounds on output logits -- that no perturbation within epsilon can flip the predicted class, providing a training-time robustness guarantee rather than a post-hoc/inference-time defense. This is a certified-training threat model, not an attack paper: no new attack is proposed.
limitations: The paper's own results show certified accuracy diverging substantially from test/PGD accuracy at larger qubit counts and class counts (worst case 39.71 percentage-point gap for interval arithmetic; certified-accuracy collapse to near-zero for 10-class, 10-qubit configurations), indicating the certification bounds become too loose to be useful at scale, a limitation the authors attribute to (but do not fully resolve with) affine arithmetic's tighter bounds. Affine arithmetic also incurs higher computational cost, which the paper states but does not quantify with timing measurements. The margin-loss target gamma=0.2 is set heuristically (based on an empirically observed margin) rather than derived analytically, and the paper notes cases (e.g., 10-qubit/10-class models) where margin loss performs worse than cross-entropy loss, without a full explanation. All evaluation is limited to VQC classifiers with amplitude embedding, RY/CNOT ansatzes, and Pauli-Z measurement on MNIST-family grayscale image datasets (MNIST, FashionMNIST, KMNIST); no evaluation on other feature maps (only briefly mentioned, e.g., angle embedding), other quantum model families, or non-image data is provided. No formal comparison against the cited classical Lipschitz-regularization or formal-verification baselines (VeriQR, semantic-abstraction verification) is run empirically -- these are discussed only qualitatively in Related Work.

quantitative_results: Interval arithmetic, MNIST 4 qubits/2 classes/2 layers, margin loss: test 96.36%, certified 95.37%, PGD 96.12% (gap 0.99%)
Interval arithmetic, MNIST 10 qubits/2 classes/2 layers, margin loss: test 98.01%, certified 58.30%, PGD 97.87% (gap 39.71%)
Affine arithmetic, MNIST 4 qubits/2 classes/8 layers, cross-entropy: test 94.61%, certified 94.42%
Affine arithmetic, MNIST 10 qubits/2 classes/8 layers, cross-entropy: test 98.11%, certified 92.34%
Affine arithmetic, MNIST 10 qubits/10 classes/8 layers, cross-entropy: test 20.82%, certified 13.83%; margin loss: test 10.84%, certified 1.50%
Affine arithmetic, MNIST 6 qubits/6 classes/8 layers: cross-entropy test 72.11%/certified 67.63%; margin loss test 82.47%/certified 77.76%
Epsilon ablation (Table III), MNIST 8 qubits/2 classes/4 layers: certified accuracy 99.15% at epsilon=0.001 down to 70.64% at epsilon=0.010, test accuracy stable ~98-99%
Epsilon ablation, MNIST 6 qubits/4 classes: certified accuracy 85.21% at epsilon=0.001 down to 4.62% at epsilon=0.010
Kappa ablation (Table IV), MNIST 6-layer model: test accuracy 80.44% (kappa=0.9) -> 89.01% (kappa=0.5) -> 89.51% (kappa=0.1)
Kappa ablation, MNIST 8 qubits/4 classes/2 layers: test accuracy decreases from 68.25% (kappa=0.9) to 55.04% (kappa=0.1), while certified accuracy increases from 36.97% to 44.60%
Affine arithmetic, MNIST worst-case certified-vs-PGD gap: 18.41% at 8 qubits/8 classes/8 layers
baselines_compared: PGD attack accuracy (empirical robustness upper bound), used within the paper's own experiments, not an external baseline method

No external certified-training or verification baseline (e.g., Lipschitz regularization, VeriQR robustness verification, semantic-abstraction formal verification) is run experimentally; these are discussed only qualitatively as related work, not benchmarked head-to-head
